content_preview.title: FTC Bans Data Broker Kochava From Selling Sensitive Location Data Without Consent, Closing a Four-Year Case
content_preview.summary: The FTC settled its 2022 lawsuit against Kochava and subsidiary Collective Data Solutions, barring the companies from selling precise location data without affirmative express consent in the most significant U.S. enforcement action against a location data broker.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The Federal Trade Commission on May 4, 2026 filed a proposed settlement in U.S. District Court for the District of Idaho that would permanently ban Idaho-based data broker Kochava Inc. and its subsidiary Collective Data Solutions (CDS) from selling precise location data without consumers' affirmative express consent. The proposed order resolves a lawsuit filed nearly four years ago and represents the most significant U.S. enforcement action against a location data broker to date, ac...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured 890-word News article covering the FTC-Kochava settlement with appropriate depth and a clear logical flow: case history, settlement terms, Kochava response, industry context. The compliance requirements (Sensitive Location Data Program, Supplier Assessment Program, deletion timelines, consumer rights, incident reporting) are accurately and clearly laid out. Tone is neutral and professional throughout. No AI self-reference. Word count (890) is within the News category range (400-1200).
editor_notes.source_verification: All 7 source snapshots read. source-0 (The Record): confirmed Collective Data Solutions subsidiary, Kochava 'pleased' quote, 'no fine' on proposed order. source-1 (Privacy Guides): confirmed Privacy Block feature, InMarket/Outlogic/Gravy Analytics/Mobilewalla 2024 bans. source-2 (AdExchanger): confirmed 2-0 vote, Dobbs/Roe v. Wade context (NOT attributed there in article). source-3 (PPC.land): confirmed case number 2:22-cv-00377-BLW, 10-year term, 90-day compliance deadline, five sensitive location categories, deletion 2-day start/30-day completion, hovering consent prohibition. source-4 (Bleeping Computer): confirmed 94B+ geo transactions, $25,000 subscription fee, stalking/discrimination/physical violence harms, InMarket/Outlogic/Gravy/Mobilewalla 2024 bans. source-5 (National Law Review): confirmed 'inherently sensitive' framing. source-6 (HIPAA Journal, archive fallback): confirmed Judge Winmill ruling denied motion to dismiss, 'always operated consistently' Kochava quote. THREE MISATTRIBUTION ISSUES IDENTIFIED: (1) The article attributes 'most significant U.S. enforcement action against a location data broker' to Privacy Guides, but this characterization does not appear in the Privacy Guides snapshot. (2) The article attributes two direct quotes from Judge Winmill — 'vast amounts of essentially non-anonymized information' and 'alleged invasion of privacy...plausibly constitutes a substantial injury to consumers' — to the HIPAA Journal, but these verbatim phrases do not appear in the HIPAA Journal snapshot (which only paraphrases the ruling without quoting it). (3) The article attributes Roe v. Wade context ('The original 2022 lawsuit was filed in the immediate aftermath of the Supreme Court's decision to overturn Roe v. Wade') to the HIPAA Journal, but this context appears only in AdExchanger (source-2), not in HIPAA Journal.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All substantive facts verified: settlement filed May 4 2026, Idaho District Court, case number 2:22-cv-00377-BLW correct (confirmed PPC.land source-3), Collective Data Solutions subsidiary correct, 2-0 vote correct, 94B+ geo transactions/month correct, $25,000 subscription fee correct, five sensitive location categories correct, 10-year term correct, 90-day compliance deadline correct, no financial penalty correct, Kochava pleased quote accurate, August 2022 lawsuit filing correct, InMarket/Outlogic/Gravy Analytics/Mobilewalla 2024 bans correct. The core reporting is solid and accurate. The Winmill quote issue is a misattribution of quotes to a paraphrase source rather than a fabricated factual claim. The court ruling denying the motion to dismiss is confirmed as factually accurate; the specific language is not reproducible from the cited source.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism or editorializing in the reporting style. The 'most significant' characterization is the only instance of evaluative language, and it reads as the contributor's inference rather than a directly sourced claim.
editor_notes.originality: No prior Kochava coverage found in the article archive. New topic, no duplicate. The case closed on May 4, 2026 and this submission was created on May 20, 2026 — timely and original.
concerns: Winmill quotes misattributed to HIPAA Journal: two direct quotes placed in quote marks and attributed 'per HIPAA Journal' ('vast amounts of essentially non-anonymized information' and 'alleged invasion of privacy...plausibly constitutes a substantial injury to consumers') do not appear verbatim in the HIPAA Journal snapshot, which only paraphrases the ruling. These phrases appear to derive from the court opinion itself but are presented as if reproduced in the cited source.

Roe v. Wade context misattributed: the sentence 'The original 2022 lawsuit was filed in the immediate aftermath of the Supreme Court's decision to overturn Roe v. Wade' is attributed to the HIPAA Journal, but this context only appears in AdExchanger (source-2), not in HIPAA Journal.
'Most significant' superlative in summary attributed to Privacy Guides in the article body, but Privacy Guides does not contain this characterization in its snapshot.
recommendations: Corrections record filed for the three misattribution issues above. All are recoverable: the underlying facts are accurate and verifiable, and none affect the headline, lead claim, or core settlement reporting.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS. The article provides accurate, well-structured coverage of a significant regulatory enforcement action. All core facts — settlement terms, compliance requirements, case history, industry context — are confirmed across sources. Three source-attribution issues are recoverable via corrections record: two Winmill quotes presented as appearing in HIPAA Journal but not present in the snapshot, Roe v. Wade context misattributed to HIPAA Journal rather than AdExchanger, and a 'most significant' superlative attributed to Privacy Guides that does not appear in the Privacy Guides snapshot. The substance of all three claims is defensible; only the attributed source is wrong. Publication with a corrections trail is appropriate.
